danish_text: Mette står op klokken seks hver morgen. Hun tager et brusebad og spiser morgenmad med sin familie. Hendes mand, Lars, laver altid kaffe, mens børnene spiser havregryn med mælk. Klokken halv otte kører Mette børnene i skole, og bagefter tager hun bussen til arbejde. Hun arbejder som kontordame i en stor virksomhed i centrum af Odense. I frokostpausen går hun ofte en tur med sin kollega, Anne. De snakker om alt muligt, mens de nyder den friske luft. Efter arbejde henter Mette børnene, og de handler ind på vejen hjem. Om aftenen laver familien mad sammen. Det er deres yndlingstid på dagen. Børnene hjælper med at dække bord, og de spiser altid sammen klokken seks. Bagefter ser de lidt fjernsyn eller spiller brætspil, inden børnene går i seng klokken otte.
english_text: Mette gets up at six o'clock every morning. She takes a shower and eats breakfast with her family. Her husband, Lars, always makes coffee while the children eat oatmeal with milk. At half past seven, Mette drives the children to school, and afterwards she takes the bus to work. She works as an office clerk in a large company in the center of Odense. During the lunch break, she often goes for a walk with her colleague, Anne. They talk about all sorts of things while they enjoy the fresh air. After work, Mette picks up the children, and they do grocery shopping on the way home. In the evening, the family cooks together. It is their favorite time of the day. The children help set the table, and they always eat together at six o'clock. Afterwards, they watch a bit of television or play board games before the children go to bed at eight.

questions.question_en: What does Lars do in the morning?

questions.question_en: What is the family's favorite time of the day?

danish_text: Danmark er et lille land, men der er meget at se og opleve. Mange turister besøger København for at se Den Lille Havfrue og Tivoli. Men der er også mange andre spændende steder i landet. Aarhus er Danmarks næststørste by og har et fantastisk kunstmuseum, der hedder ARoS. I Nordjylland kan man besøge Skagen, hvor Skagerrak og Kattegat mødes. Det er et populært sted for kunstnere, fordi lyset er helt specielt der. Hvis man kan lide natur, er Bornholm et godt valg. Øen ligger i Østersøen og er kendt for sine smukke klippekyster og røgerier, hvor man kan smage den berømte røgede sild. Man kan nemt rejse rundt i Danmark med tog. DSB, det danske jernbaneselskab, har forbindelser til alle de store byer. Det er også muligt at leje en bil eller tage færgen til de mange danske øer.
english_text: Denmark is a small country, but there is a lot to see and experience. Many tourists visit Copenhagen to see The Little Mermaid and Tivoli. But there are also many other exciting places in the country. Aarhus is Denmark's second-largest city and has a fantastic art museum called ARoS. In North Jutland, you can visit Skagen, where the Skagerrak and the Kattegat meet. It is a popular place for artists because the light is very special there. If you like nature, Bornholm is a good choice. The island is located in the Baltic Sea and is known for its beautiful rocky coasts and smokehouses where you can taste the famous smoked herring. You can easily travel around Denmark by train. DSB, the Danish railway company, has connections to all the major cities. It is also possible to rent a car or take the ferry to the many Danish islands.

danish_text: Dansk madkultur har ændret sig meget i de sidste årtier. Traditionelt har danskerne spist mange kartofler, flæskesteg og rugbrød. Smørrebrød er nok den mest kendte danske ret i udlandet. Det er et stykke rugbrød med forskelligt pålæg, for eksempel sild, leverpostej eller roastbeef. I dag er det nye nordiske køkken blevet meget populært. Noma i København har flere gange været kåret som verdens bedste restaurant. Det nye nordiske køkken fokuserer på lokale og sæsonbestemte råvarer. Mange danskere spiser stadig frokost med rugbrød på arbejdet, men aftensmaden er blevet mere international. Det er helt normalt at lave pasta, sushi eller indisk mad hjemme. Danskerne er også glade for at gå ud og spise. Der findes mange forskellige restauranter i de større byer. Noget, der er typisk dansk, er fredagsslik. Hver fredag køber mange familier slik og snacks, som de nyder sammen foran fjernsynet.
english_text: Danish food culture has changed a lot in recent decades. Traditionally, Danes have eaten a lot of potatoes, roast pork, and rye bread. Smørrebrød is probably the most well-known Danish dish abroad. It is a piece of rye bread with various toppings, for example herring, liver pate, or roast beef. Today, the New Nordic cuisine has become very popular. Noma in Copenhagen has been voted the world's best restaurant several times. The New Nordic cuisine focuses on local and seasonal ingredients. Many Danes still eat lunch with rye bread at work, but dinner has become more international. It is completely normal to make pasta, sushi, or Indian food at home. Danes also enjoy eating out. There are many different restaurants in the larger cities. Something that is typically Danish is Friday candy. Every Friday, many families buy candy and snacks, which they enjoy together in front of the television.

danish_text: Den danske arbejdskultur adskiller sig på mange måder fra andre landes. Et af de mest bemærkelsesværdige træk er den flade hierarkiske struktur. I danske virksomheder er det normalt, at medarbejdere tiltaler deres chef ved fornavn, og alle forventes at bidrage med idéer uanset deres position. Begrebet 'work-life balance' tages meget alvorligt. De fleste danskere arbejder fra klokken otte til seksten og forlader kontoret til tiden. Overarbejde ses ikke nødvendigvis som et tegn på engagement, men snarere som dårlig tidsstyring. Fleksibilitet er også centralt. Mange virksomheder tilbyder hjemmearbejde og fleksible arbejdstider, især efter pandemien. Tillid spiller en afgørende rolle i det danske arbejdsliv. Ledere stoler på, at medarbejderne udfører deres opgaver selvstændigt, uden behov for konstant overvågning. Til gengæld forventes det, at man er ærlig om eventuelle problemer og tager ansvar for sit eget arbejde. Fagforeningerne har historisk set haft stor indflydelse på arbejdsmarkedet, og overenskomstforhandlinger er en vigtig del af det danske system. Denne model, der kaldes flexicurity, kombinerer fleksibilitet for arbejdsgiverne med sikkerhed for arbejdstagerne.
english_text: Danish work culture differs in many ways from that of other countries. One of the most notable features is the flat hierarchical structure. In Danish companies, it is normal for employees to address their boss by first name, and everyone is expected to contribute ideas regardless of their position. The concept of 'work-life balance' is taken very seriously. Most Danes work from eight to four and leave the office on time. Overtime is not necessarily seen as a sign of commitment, but rather as poor time management. Flexibility is also central. Many companies offer remote work and flexible working hours, especially after the pandemic. Trust plays a crucial role in Danish working life. Managers trust that employees carry out their tasks independently, without the need for constant supervision. In return, it is expected that one is honest about any problems and takes responsibility for one's own work. Trade unions have historically had a great influence on the labor market, and collective bargaining is an important part of the Danish system. This model, called flexicurity, combines flexibility for employers with security for employees.

questions.question_da: Hvad er usædvanligt ved den hierarkiske struktur i danske virksomheder?
questions.question_en: What is unusual about the hierarchical structure in Danish companies?
options: Strukturen er flad, og medarbejdere tiltaler chefen ved fornavn
Kun chefer må komme med idéer

danish_text: Danmarks klima er tempereret og præget af landets beliggenhed mellem Nordsøen og Østersøen. Vejret kan skifte hurtigt, og det er ikke ualmindeligt at opleve flere årstider på én dag. Vinteren i Danmark er generelt mild sammenlignet med andre skandinaviske lande, men den kan føles bider kold på grund af den fugtighed og vind, der kommer fra havet. Temperaturen ligger typisk mellem nul og fem grader i vintermånederne, og solen går ned allerede omkring klokken halvfire i december. Denne mørke periode er grunden til, at danskerne har udviklet begrebet hygge, hvor man tænder stearinlys og nyder varme drikke indendørs. Foråret ankommer ofte sent, og det kan stadig fryse i april. Men når solen endelig bryder frem, forvandler landet sig. Kirsebærtræerne blomstrer, og folk strømmer ud i parkerne. Sommeren er kort men intens, med temperaturer mellem tyve og femogtyve grader og lyse aftener, der varer til klokken ti. Efteråret bringer smukke farver og høststorme, der fejer hen over det flade landskab. Mange danskere nyder at gå ture i skovene for at samle svampe og nyde de gyldne blade.
english_text: Denmark's climate is temperate and influenced by the country's location between the North Sea and the Baltic Sea. The weather can change quickly, and it is not unusual to experience several seasons in one day. Winter in Denmark is generally mild compared to other Scandinavian countries, but it can feel bitterly cold due to the humidity and wind that comes from the sea. The temperature typically ranges between zero and five degrees during the winter months, and the sun sets as early as around half past three in December. This dark period is the reason why Danes have developed the concept of hygge, where one lights candles and enjoys warm drinks indoors. Spring often arrives late, and it can still freeze in April. But when the sun finally breaks through, the country transforms. Cherry trees blossom, and people stream out into the parks. Summer is short but intense, with temperatures between twenty and twenty-five degrees and bright evenings that last until ten o'clock. Autumn brings beautiful colors and harvest storms that sweep across the flat landscape. Many Danes enjoy walking in the forests to gather mushrooms and enjoy the golden leaves.

questions.question_da: Hvorfor kan vinteren føles koldere, end temperaturen antyder?
questions.question_en: Why can winter feel colder than the temperature suggests?
options: På grund af fugtighed og vind fra havet
På grund af mangel på opvarmning
